sanskrit_devanagari:
किं कर्म किमकर्मेति कवयोऽप्यत्र मोहिताः ।
तत्ते कर्म प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात् ॥ १६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
kiṁ karma kim akarmeti kavayo 'py atra mohitāḥ |
tat te karma pravakṣyāmi yaj jñātvā mokṣyase 'śubhāt || 16 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Even the intelligent are bewildered in determining what is action and what is inaction. Now I shall explain to you what action is, knowing which you shall be liberated from all misfortune.
Even the intelligent are bewildered in determining what is action and what is inaction. Now I shall explain to you what action is, knowing which you shall be liberated from all misfortune.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1866 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Action in Kṛṣṇa consciousness has to be executed in accord with the examples of previous bona fide devotees. This is recommended in the fifteenth verse. Why such action should not be independent will be explained in the text to follow.To act in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, one has to follow the leadership o…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.16 Kavayah api, even the intelligent; mohitah, are confounded in this subject of action etc.; iti atra, as to; kim karma, what is action; and kim akarma, what is inaction. Therefore, pravaksyami, I shall tell; te, you; of karma, action; akarma ca, as also of inaction; jnatva, by knowing; yat, whic…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-16-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's preparatory statement: the matter of action vs. inaction is so subtle that even the seers are confused. The verse signals that the teaching that follows (4.18 in particular) is one that requires careful attention. The fruit (mokṣa from aśubha) is named to establish stakes.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.16 What is the form of the action which should be done by an aspirant for liberation? And what is non-action? Knowledge about the true nature of the acting self, is spoken of as non-action.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-16-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as Krishna explicitly addressing the difficulty: distinguishing action from inaction is hard even for the wise. The verse establishes that what follows is doctrinally important — the teaching that releases from the inauspicious (the bondage of karma).

positions.rendering: Even the intelligent are bewildered in determining what is action and what is inaction
Even the seers (kavi) are confused — the matter is genuinely subtle
The wise themselves find this difficult; therefore Krishna declares it explicitly

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three read mohitāḥ honestly as 'confused' — Krishna is not being rhetorical. The matter genuinely escapes ordinary discrimination. The verse's role is to motivate close attention to 4.17-4.18.

literal_meaning: What is action and what is inaction — even the wise are confused here; I shall declare to you that action knowing which you will be freed from the inauspicious.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots into the action-in-inaction discussion. He acknowledges that even kavi (the wise, the seers) are confused about the distinction between karma and akarma. The implication: this is not a trivial dyad. Krishna will now declare the precise teaching that resolves the confusion. The verse sets up 4.17 (the threefold typology) and 4.18 (the headline seeing).
